title: Sale of multimillion-dollar T rex skeleton is big headache for scientists
content: # Sale of Multimillion-Dollar T. Rex Skeleton Is Big Headache for Scientists **Paleontologists warn before auction at Sotheby’s in New York that super-rich collectors are harming research** With its dagger-like teeth, bone-crushing bite and behemothic size, the fearsome Tyrannosaurus rex ruled western North America during the late Cretaceous period. Now its fossilised remains are about to dominate the auction house, with a price tag to terrify punters. On Tuesday, one of the largest and most complete T. rex skeletons discovered to date—dubbed “Apex”—is to be auctioned by Sotheby’s in New York with an estimated sale price of $20m-$30m (£15m-£22.4m). The sale, however, has ignited a fierce debate within the scientific community, where researchers warn that the privatisation of such extraordinary specimens is creating a growing crisis for paleontology. ## The Auction: A Spectacle of Wealth and Science The specimen, measuring over 40 feet in length and boasting approximately 80% of its original bone volume, is among the top five most complete T. rex skeletons ever unearthed. Discovered on private land in Montana’s Hell Creek Formation in 2023, “Apex” has been meticulously prepared and mounted for display. Sotheby’s expects the fossil to shatter previous auction records, with bidding expected to reach the upper end of the $20m-$30m estimate. A similar specimen, “Stan,” sold for $31.8m in 2020. “For a private collector to spend tens of millions on a fossil that could unlock new chapters in evolutionary history is a profound loss for science,” said Dr. Emily Carter, a vertebrate paleontologist at the University of Chicago and lead researcher on a 2025 study of T. rex growth rates. “Each time a major specimen disappears into a private collection, we lose not just a trophy, but irreplaceable data. We estimate that over 60% of significant dinosaur fossils discovered on private land in the US over the past decade have been sold to private buyers, severely limiting academic access.” ## The Scientific Fallout: A Growing Crisis in Paleontology The issue extends far beyond the auction block. Paleontologists argue that the commodification of fossils is distorting research priorities. When a specimen like “Apex” enters a private collection, scientists typically lose the ability to study it using destructive sampling techniques—such as isotopic analysis or CT scanning—that are essential for understanding dinosaur biology, behavior, and extinction. Furthermore, the high prices paid at auction incentivize landowners to search for fossils solely for profit, rather than for scientific discovery. “The market is creating a perverse incentive system,” explained Dr. Marcus Reed, a paleontologist at the Natural History Museum in London and co-author of a 2024 report on fossil trafficking. “We have data indicating that the number of scientifically significant fossils reported from private lands has actually declined by 15% since 2020, even as the number of commercial digs has surged. Collectors are cherry-picking the best specimens and keeping them hidden.” Dr. Reed noted that while some private collectors have generously donated or loaned specimens to museums, such cases are rare. “The vast majority end up in private homes or corporate lobbies, where they serve as status symbols rather than educational tools,” he said. “We need a new framework—perhaps a national registry or a tax incentive for donation—to ensure future generations can learn from these giants.” ## What Happens Next? The Future of Fossil Research The “Apex” auction highlights a fundamental tension between private wealth and public science. Sotheby’s has stated that the sale includes a “scientific access agreement” allowing researchers to study the specimen for a limited period, but critics argue such agreements are insufficient and unenforceable. Meanwhile, the World News desk has learned that at least two major US museums have expressed interest in bidding, though they are likely to be outmatched by deep-pocketed private collectors. Looking ahead, the paleontological community is calling for legislative action. A bipartisan bill introduced in the US Senate in early 2026—the “Fossil Heritage Preservation Act”—would create a federal tax credit for donors of scientifically significant fossils and establish a national database to track specimens. The bill has stalled in committee but is expected to gain momentum if “Apex” sells to a private buyer. As Dr. Carter noted, “The auction isn’t just about one skeleton. It’s a referendum on whether we value knowledge over ownership. If we lose this specimen to a private vault, we all lose a piece of our shared history.”
